ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general soba noodle dish entry; no specific cha soba (green tea soba) entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers soba/buckwheat noodles generically; no dedicated cha soba code exists. Green tea variant mapped to closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for cha soba or soba noodle dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of cha soba is approximately 250 g (one bowl), providing around 155 kcal, 9.1 g protein, 25.6 g carbohydrates, and 1.8 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Cha Soba — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (F05060019)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05060019 for Cha Soba. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cha soba (green tea soba) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 46 is based on the closest analogous food: soba noodles (buckwheat noodles, boiled), which have a published GI of approximately 46 (low GI) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The green tea (matcha) component does not materially alter the GI of the noodle base. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 46 × 10.22 g × 2.5 (250 g serving) / 100 = 11.75 / 2.5 ≈ 4.7 per 100 g equivalent portion. GL per 250 g serving = 46 × 25.55 / 100 ≈ 11.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 203 mg/100 g; soy-based dipping sauce substantially increases total sodium intake per serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume cha soba with dipping sauce used sparingly or replaced with a low-sodium soy sauce alternative, as the noodle base alone contributes approximately 508 mg sodium per 250 g serving and the accompanying soy dipping broth can add a further 400–600 mg sodium, potentially exceeding 50% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium target in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~46) from buckwheat base; moderate carbohydrate load (10.2 g/100 g); low sugar (1.1 g/100 g); matcha polyphenols may modestly improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cha soba is a suitable noodle choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its low GI (~46) and moderate carbohydrate content; a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 25.6 g carbohydrates, which is manageable within a structured meal plan when paired with protein (tofu, egg) and non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose response, and sweet dipping sauces with added mirin or sugar should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low fat (0.72 g/100 g), low saturated fat (not detected in HPB record), buckwheat rutin content, and matcha catechins with evidence-based cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cha soba is a heart-friendly noodle dish with very low total fat (0.72 g/100 g) and negligible saturated fat; the buckwheat component provides rutin, a flavonoid associated with improved vascular tone and reduced LDL oxidation, while matcha catechins (EGCG) have demonstrated modest reductions in LDL cholesterol in clinical trials, making this dish appropriate for cardiovascular disease management when sodium from the dipping sauce is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Matcha catechins and buckwheat rutin provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols; dish is low in saturated fat and free of nightshades
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cha soba is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the matcha component is rich in catechins (particularly EGCG) with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties, buckwheat provides rutin with anti-inflammatory activity, the dish is nightshade-free, and high-sodium dipping sauces should be avoided to prevent worsening of RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cha soba (茶そば) is a Japanese noodle dish made from soba noodles infused with matcha (green tea powder), giving the noodles a distinctive pale green colour and a subtle earthy, grassy flavour. The noodles are made from a blend of buckwheat flour and wheat flour, with matcha incorporated into the dough. They are typically boiled until tender with a slightly firm, chewy texture, then served either cold (zaru style) or warm in a light dashi-based broth. The dish is traditionally garnished with toasted nori (dried seaweed), freshly grated wasabi, and sliced green onions, and is accompanied by a soy sauce-based dipping broth (tsuyu). Per 100 g, cha soba provides 62 kcal, 3.63 g protein, 0.72 g fat, 10.22 g carbohydrates, 1.09 g sugar, 0.97 g dietary fibre, and 203 mg sodium, with 16 mg calcium and 0.89 mg iron. The buckwheat base gives cha soba a low glycemic index of approximately 46, making it a preferred noodle choice over refined wheat noodles for blood glucose management. The matcha component contributes catechins, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and modest cardioprotective effects in clinical research. Buckwheat provides rutin, a flavonoid associated with improved vascular tone. The primary clinical caution is sodium: the noodle base alone contributes approximately 508 mg sodium per 250 g serving, and the soy dipping sauce can add a further 400–600 mg, making sodium management critical for hypertensive patients. Cha soba is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, but contains gluten (wheat and buckwheat) and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05060019 at confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous food (soba noodles, GI ~46) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Cultural and culinary facts are well-established for this Japanese dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo record F05060019 for Cha Soba, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for cha soba with these micronutrient values as of 2026-04-16. The input record incorrectly classified primary_cuisine as 'western'; corrected to 'Japanese' based on established culinary origin. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate based on soba noodles (buckwheat noodles, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for cha soba exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per 250 g serving ≈ 11.8 g. All core macros (protein_g 3.63, fat_g 0.72, carbohydrate_g 10.22) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
